title: Daily Actions

entries.notes: Root: buklat. Patient-focus: ibuklat. Confirmed in the Kapampangan Dictionary (Forman 1971) and polytranslator. Kabuklat = break of dawn.
Root: sara. Perfective: menyara/manyara. Confirmed in Kapampangan Verbs Flashcards (Quizlet) and multiple sources showing menyara/manyara/magsara conjugations.

examples.en: I am washing clothes in the water.

entries.notes: Confirmed Kapampangan word for washing clothes by hand. Documented in 'Views from the Pampang' blog and kapampangantraveller.com. Related to the occupation of a laundrywomen (mamipi).

examples.en: She cleans the house every day.

entries.notes: Confirmed in Sunstar.com.ph article 'Kalinisan ding Kapampangan'. Also seen as linisan (patient-focus). Root: linis (cleanliness).

examples.kp: Magaral ya i Jose king iskuwela.

examples.en: I am studying my lesson now.

entries.notes: Confirmed Kapampangan verb on Wiktionary (en.wiktionary.org/wiki/magaral). Perfective: megaral/migaral. Formed from mag- + aral. Also evidenced in Kapampangan grammar texts as 'megaral yang ditac'.

examples.kp: Mamyalung la reng anak king labuad.

examples.en: I want to play with them.

entries.notes: Root: pyalung (game/play). Pyalung is the Kapampangan word for 'game' confirmed in academic research ('The Pyalung Kapampangan') and Sunstar.com.ph. Mamyalung is the actor-focus verbal form. Also seen as mamialung.
Confirmed in Kapampangan phrasebook (Wikivoyage) and polytranslator English-Kapampangan. 'Saupan yu ku pu' is a documented Kapampangan phrase. Also seen as 'sopan mu ku' (help me). Root: saup.

examples.kp: Antayan tamu ya karing aduang oras.
examples.en: We will wait for two hours.

entries.notes: Confirmed in Kapampangan phrasebook (Wikivoyage) for wait-related forms. 'Nanung panayan mu?' (for what do you wait?) evidences the antay/panay root in Kapampangan. Antayan = patient-focus form of antay (wait).

examples.kp: Magkanta ya ing babai king entabladu.

entries.notes: Confirmed Kapampangan form for 'to sing'. Used in Kapampangan folk songs and Tagaloglang community examples. Root: kanta (song, from Spanish). The mag- prefix is the Kapampangan actor-focus form. Confirmed at tagaloglang.com Kapampangan section.

examples.en: I want to dance with you.

entries.notes: Confirmed Kapampangan on Wiktionary (noun and verb: 'a sequence of rhythmic steps... to dance, to sway'). Also confirmed in tagaloglang.com and the blog 'Views from the Pampang: Shall We Dance? Terak Kapampangan'.

examples.kp: Tawa ya ing anak nung dingna na.
examples.en: The child laughs when he hears it.

entries.notes: Confirmed as Kapampangan verb meaning 'laugh' in multiple sources including Wikivoyage phrasebook and search results. Also appears in the Kapampangan compound 'iyak-tawa'. Root: tawa.

examples.en: She cries when she is tired.

entries.notes: Confirmed Kapampangan verb in tagaloglang.com dictionary (kumyak = umiyak = cry). Also in Kapampangan Verbs Flashcards. Actor-focus form of root kiak/kyak.
Confirmed in polytranslator English-Kapampangan dictionary: denggan = 'listen'. Patient-focus form. Root: denge/dengge. Also confirmed in Wikivoyage phrasebook as the Kapampangan word for listen.

Confirmed in tagaloglang.com dictionary (sumabi = magsalita = speak). Also in kapampangantraveller.com. Root: sabi. Actor-focus: sumabi. Patient-focus: sabian (which is in the avoid-list as a separate entry).

Well-confirmed Kapampangan verb. Attested in tagaloglang.com dictionary, Sunstar.com.ph article ('Kalinisan ding Kapampangan'), and kapampangantraveller.com. The saying 'ya'ring mandilu, manggugu' is a famous Kapampangan expression.

examples.en: She sweeps the floor every day.

entries.notes: Confirmed in Sunstar.com.ph article 'Kalinisan ding Kapampangan' (manaya = to sweep). Related to tidiness and cleanliness in Kapampangan home life.

lessons.title: Open & Close
lessons.intro: Learn the verbs for opening, closing, and sweeping: buklat, sara, and manaya.

exercises.prompt: Sara me ing bintana. → Root verb: ____ (to close)
____ ya ing lapag aldo-aldo. (She sweeps the floor every day.)

lessons.title: Wash & Clean
lessons.intro: Learn three hygiene verbs: mamipi (wash clothes), mangalinis (clean), and mandilu (bathe).

accepted: she cleans the house every day.
she cleans the house every day
he cleans the house every day.
he cleans the house every day

exercises.prompt: ____ ya ing inda aldo-aldo. (Mother does laundry every day.)
____ tamu king kusina. (Let's clean the kitchen.)

lessons.title: Learn & Play
lessons.intro: Learn three everyday activity verbs: magaral (study), mamyalung (play), and saupan (help).

exercises.prompt: Magaral ya i Jose king iskuwela.
Mamyalung la reng anak king labuad.
____ me ing ina mu. (Help your mother.)
____ ku leksyon ku ngeni. (I am studying my lesson now.)

lessons.title: Wait & Speak
lessons.intro: Learn three communication verbs: antayan (wait/wait for), sumabi (speak/say), and denggan (listen).

exercises.prompt: ____ me ku deti. (Wait for me here.)
____ me ing sabian ku. (Listen to what I say.)

lessons.title: Sing & Dance
lessons.intro: Kapampangans are known for their love of arts! Learn: magkanta (sing), terak (dance), and tawa (laugh).

exercises.prompt: ____ ya ing babai king entabladu. (The woman sings on stage.)

lessons.title: Feelings in Action
lessons.intro: This lesson covers the remaining action verb: kumyak (to cry/weep). Review and practice all 16 daily action words.

exercises.prompt: ____ ya ing anak nung dingna na. (The child laughs when he hears it.)

sources: Wiktionary: magaral (Kapampangan verb, to study) — https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/magaral
Wiktionary: terak (Kapampangan noun/verb, dance/to dance) — https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/terak
Tagaloglang.com Kapampangan Dictionary (kumyak=cry, sumabi=speak, terak=dance, mandilu=bathe) — https://www.tagaloglang.com/kapampangan-dictionary/
Polytranslator English-Kapampangan Dictionary (denggan=listen, saupan=help, buklat=open, magaral=study) — https://www.polytranslator.com/dictionary/kapampangan/

Sunstar.com.ph — Garcia: Kalinisan ding Kapampangan (mandilu=bathe, mangalinis=clean, manaya=sweep, linisan=clean) — https://www.sunstar.com.ph/more-articles/garcia-kalinisan-ding-kapampangan-kapampangan-cleanliness-
Kapampangan Phrasebook — Wikivoyage (buklat=open, sara=close, saupan=help, denggan=listen, sumabi=speak, mandilu=bathe, antayan=wait) — https://en.wikivoyage.org/wiki/Kapampangan_phrasebook
Kapampangantraveller.com — Learn Kapampangan (mamipi=wash clothes, sumabi=speak, pyalung=play/game) — https://kapampangantraveller.com/blog/
Views from the Pampang blog — MAMIPI: Livelihood in Laundry (mamipi=Kapampangan laundrywomen/wash clothes) — http://viewsfromthepampang.blogspot.com/2009/05/147-mamipi-livelihood-in-laundry.html
Forman, Michael L. (1971). Kapampangan Dictionary. University of Hawai'i Press. (buklat: ibuklat me ing libro mu; buklatan=open something)
Sunstar.com.ph — 'Nanung panayan mu?' evidencing antay/panay root for wait in Kapampangan
confidenceNotes: HIGH confidence (3+ independent Kapampangan sources): terak (dance — Wiktionary, tagaloglang, Views from the Pampang blog), magaral (study — Wiktionary entry, grammar texts, Kapampangan Traveller), kumyak (cry — tagaloglang confirmed kumyak=umiyak), mandilu (bathe — tagaloglang, sunstar.com.ph, kapampangantraveller), sumabi (speak — tagaloglang, kapampangantraveller, multiple phrase sources), saupan (help — Wikivoyage phrasebook, polytranslator, tagaloglang phrases). MEDIUM confidence (2 sources): buklat (open — polytranslator + Forman dictionary; 'ibuklat me ing libro mu' attested), sara (close — Quizlet Kapampangan Verbs + Wikivoyage: 'Eme sasara ing pasbul'), tawa (laugh — Wikivoyage phrasebook + multiple search results), denggan (listen — polytranslator + Wikivoyage phrasebook), mangalinis (clean — sunstar.com.ph article + Wikivoyage phrasebook linisin), mamipi (wash clothes — kapampangantraveller + Views from the Pampang dedicated article), manaya (sweep — sunstar.com.ph cleanliness article), magkanta (sing — tagaloglang community use + Kapampangan folk song context). LOWER confidence, retained due to multiple attestations: mamyalung (play — pyalung confirmed as Kapampangan game noun in academic research; mamyalung verbal form confirmed in kapampangantraveller and josephjames007 blog), antayan (wait — antay/panay root evidenced in Kapampangan sources; antayan as patient-focus form is structurally consistent). EXCLUDED: hugas (wash dishes — primarily Tagalog; Kapampangan cognate is 'uyas' per one source but insufficient attestation), bukas (open — Tagalog form not confirmed as Kapampangan), laro (play — Tagalog form; Kapampangan uses pyalung/mamyalung).
